subject: ap-government
theme: The Constitution protects individual freedoms from government overreach through the Bill of Rights, which selective incorporation has extended to state governments, while the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment has driven an ongoing expansion of civil rights for historically marginalized groups.
core_concepts: Civil liberties are protections from government infringement on individual freedoms (Bill of Rights); civil rights are equal treatment guarantees requiring affirmative government action — the two concepts are distinct but often tested together on the AP exam.

Selective incorporation applies most Bill of Rights protections to state governments through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause — key cases include Gideon v. Wainwright (6th Amendment right to counsel) and McDonald v. Chicago (2nd Amendment).

The First Amendment's Establishment Clause and Free Exercise Clause create a 'wall of separation' — Engel v. Vitale (1962) banned school-sponsored prayer; Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) protected Amish families from compulsory education, showing how the two clauses can conflict.

Student free speech is protected but limited: Tinker v. Des Moines (1969) protected symbolic protest (armbands) unless substantially disruptive; Bethel School District v. Fraser (1986) allowed schools to restrict vulgar speech; Hazelwood (1988) allowed editorial control of school publications.

The Fourth Amendment's exclusionary rule, established through Mapp v. Ohio (1961), bars evidence obtained through unconstitutional searches — courts apply a balancing test between Fourth Amendment protections and law enforcement needs.

The Equal Protection Clause generates a three-tier system of judicial review: strict scrutiny for racial classifications (compelling interest + narrow tailoring), intermediate scrutiny for gender, and rational basis for economic regulations.

Brown v. Board of Education (1954) overturned Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine — the civil rights movement that followed used both litigation and civil disobedience (MLK's Letter from Birmingham Jail) to dismantle de jure segregation, leading to the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Voting Rights Act of 1965.

key_terms.definition: Protections from government infringement on individual freedoms, found primarily in the Bill of Rights (e.g., freedom of speech, religion, due process)
Equal treatment under the law and protection from discrimination, typically requiring government action to guarantee equal opportunity for all groups
The legal doctrine by which the Fourteenth Amendment's due process clause has been used to apply most of the Bill of Rights to state governments
The First Amendment protection that prohibits the government from establishing an official religion or favoring one religion over others
The constitutional requirement that government must follow fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property
A judicial doctrine holding that evidence obtained through unconstitutional police searches cannot be used in court
The constitutional right, recognized in Griswold v. Connecticut (1965), that the government cannot intrude into certain intimate personal decisions, though not explicitly stated in the Constitution
The Fourteenth Amendment clause requiring states to provide equal legal protections to all people within their jurisdictions
The highest level of judicial review, applied to government actions that classify by race or national origin — the government must show a compelling interest and narrowly tailored means
The doctrine that government cannot prohibit the publication of information in advance; censorship before publication is presumptively unconstitutional
The 1962 Supreme Court case that ruled school-sponsored prayer in public schools violated the Establishment Clause
The 1969 Supreme Court case establishing that students do not 'shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate' — symbolic speech is protected unless it causes substantial disruption
The 1919 Supreme Court case that established the 'clear and present danger' test for evaluating government limits on free speech during wartime
The 1963 Supreme Court case that guaranteed the right to an attorney in all felony cases, requiring states to provide counsel to defendants who cannot afford it
The 1954 Supreme Court case that unanimously overturned Plessy v. Ferguson, ruling that racially segregated public schools were inherently unequal and violated the Equal Protection Clause

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests civil liberties and civil rights together — always distinguish between them (civil liberties = protection FROM government; civil rights = equal treatment BY government), and know the three-tier scrutiny framework: strict scrutiny for race, intermediate for gender, rational basis for economic classifications.
